request_details.how_will_the_funds_be_used:
We are requesting funds to support the expansion of the Stoughton Community Youth Garden Initiative at the Stoughton Community Center. Specifically, the RoundUP Program donation will be used in the following detailed ways:

1. Garden Infrastructure and Supplies (~$1,200):
- Purchase of 6 additional cedar raised beds to accommodate our growing waitlist of youth participants (currently 27 students, grades 4–8, from Kegonsa Elementary and River Bluff Middle School).
- Organic soil and compost to fill the new beds (approximately 5 cubic yards) and to amend existing beds that have become compacted over the last two seasons.
- Replacement of worn-out hand tools sized for children (trowels, hand rakes, watering cans, and gloves in multiple sizes). Many of our current tools were donated secondhand and have become rusted or broken.
- Drip irrigation supplies (tubing, connectors, and a timer) to reduce water waste and ensure consistent watering when volunteers are unavailable during hot summer weeks.

2. Educational Materials and Program Support (~$600):
- Age-appropriate curriculum materials on gardening, composting, pollinators, and basic nutrition, including laminated activity cards, garden journals for each participant, and simple plant identification guides.
- A modest stipend for two local high school students (through the Stoughton High School Service Learning program) who will serve as paid youth mentors for 6 weeks in the summer. This will make participation more accessible for them, as both currently work part-time jobs to help their families.
- Printing costs for bilingual (English/Spanish) handouts and flyers explaining garden activities, schedules, and food safety guidelines for families.

3. Community Harvest and Access (~$400):
- Reusable produce bags, labels, and basic food-safe containers so that families can easily take home harvested vegetables.
- A small outdoor produce stand (simple wooden shelf with a covered top and signage) to host our "Free Garden Share" table on Tuesday and Thursday evenings. This will allow any community member to pick up fresh produce on a first-come, first-served basis.
- Basic canning and preservation supplies (jars, lids, and ingredients like vinegar and salt) for one or two simple introductory workshops on how to safely preserve tomatoes, cucumbers, and beans.

4. Accessibility and Inclusion (~$300):
- Two adaptive garden tools with extended handles for grandparents and adult volunteers with mobility limitations, as well as one adjustable-height garden stool.
- Materials to build one lower-height raised bed that is more accessible for younger children and individuals who use wheelchairs or have difficulty bending.

In short, the funds will be fully dedicated to tangible, community-facing resources that directly support youth engagement, food production, and equitable access to fresh produce, with a strong emphasis on education, safety, and inclusion.
request_details.what_are_the_benefits_to_the_stoughton_c:
The Stoughton Community Youth Garden Initiative directly benefits the Stoughton community in several interconnected ways: food access, youth development, community-building, and environmental education.

1. Improved Access to Fresh, Healthy Food:
- Many of our participating families live in apartments or homes without enough yard space or sunlight for a garden. By expanding the number of raised beds and improving irrigation, we can grow more produce and share it more consistently.
- During the 2024 growing season, our small pilot garden produced an estimated 350 pounds of vegetables (tomatoes, cucumbers, beans, kale, lettuce, zucchini, herbs). Even with our limited space, we were able to send home at least one bag of produce per week with each participating family.
- With the additional beds and irrigation funded by this request, we conservatively estimate increasing production to 600–700 pounds of produce in the next season. This will support not only enrolled youth but also community members who visit our "Free Garden Share" table.
- Several parents have told us that the free vegetables help them stretch their grocery budgets, especially at the end of the month. One parent, Maria (a single mother of three who lives on South Page Street), shared that her kids were excited to eat cherry tomatoes and green beans because they grew them themselves, and that this reduced their requests for fast food.

2. Youth Engagement, Education, and Positive Activities:
- The garden provides a safe, supervised, and constructive after-school and summer activity for local youth, particularly those who may not have the resources to participate in fee-based sports or camps.
- Students learn practical skills: how to plan and plant a garden bed, how to read seed packets, how to recognize healthy soil, and how to identify common Wisconsin pollinators. We also integrate simple STEM lessons around measuring plant growth, tracking weather patterns, and understanding basic ecology.
- We have seen shy students become more confident as they take responsibility for tasks like leading a watering schedule, explaining plant names to their peers, or showing their parents what they have grown. One student, Liam, who started the program barely speaking above a whisper, now proudly gives short "garden tours" to visitors during our open house days.

3. Strengthening Community Connections and Volunteerism:
- The garden brings together people who might not otherwise meet: retired residents, high school volunteers, parents, elementary and middle school students, and community members walking by the community center.
- We host monthly "Family Garden Nights" where relatives can join youth participants for simple activities like planting seedlings, painting garden signs, or learning a new recipe. These evenings have turned into informal gatherings where neighbors share tips, recipes, and sometimes even extra seeds or tools.
- Our small "Free Garden Share" stand is intentionally open to anyone, no questions asked. This reduces stigma around receiving free food and encourages residents to see the garden as a shared resource.

4. Environmental Stewardship and Local Pride:
- Youth participants learn about composting, water conservation, and pollinator-friendly planting. For example, last year they helped design and plant a small pollinator strip with native flowers, and they loved seeing the increase in bees and butterflies.
- By installing drip irrigation, we model responsible water use and can talk concretely with students about why this matters during increasingly hot summers.
- The garden beautifies an underused patch of land next to the community center, turning what was previously a weedy, compacted area into a colorful, productive, and welcoming space.

5. Long-Term Impact and Sustainability:
- Expanding the garden with durable raised beds, basic infrastructure, and a structured mentorship model helps ensure this is not just a one-year project. Our goal is to build a program that can run year after year with a combination of grants, community donations, and volunteer support.
- The inclusion of high school youth mentors creates a leadership pipeline: younger students see older peers in positive, responsible roles, and mentors gain practical experience in teaching, communication, and community service.
- Over time, we hope some of our youth participants will start home container gardens or get involved in other local environmental and food-related efforts, carrying these skills and values into adulthood.

In sum, this request will help the Stoughton community by increasing access to healthy food, providing meaningful learning and leadership opportunities for youth, fostering stronger intergenerational connections, and building a visible, shared space that reflects local pride and care for our environment.
request_details.what_other_information_would_you_like_to:
The Stoughton Community Youth Garden Initiative grew out of conversations among parents, teachers, and staff at the Stoughton Community Center who noticed that kids were increasingly interested in where their food comes from but had very few hands-on opportunities to explore that interest.

Program Background:
- In 2023, we started with just three small raised beds and a handful of volunteers. We learned a lot that first year—everything from the importance of setting up a watering schedule before summer vacation to labeling plants clearly so kids could remember what they planted.
- We discovered that many of our youth participants had never tasted certain vegetables fresh from the garden. For example, during a taste test last July, at least half the group had never tried raw snap peas, and several were surprised that "carrots come from under the ground and not just from a bag." These seemingly small moments have a big impact on their curiosity and openness to trying new foods.

Partnerships and Support:
- We work informally with staff at Kegonsa Elementary and River Bluff Middle School, who help us identify students who would especially benefit from the program—children who might not be involved in other extracurriculars or who could use a positive, low-pressure environment to build social skills.
- The Stoughton Community Center provides us with access to water, storage space for tools, and the land for the garden itself. We also collaborate with a local Master Gardener volunteer, "Mr. Dave," who comes twice a month to offer guidance on pest management and crop rotation.
- We have received small in-kind donations in the form of seeds, a few tools, and compost from local residents, but we have not yet had a stable source of funding to expand or replace worn-out materials.

Equity and Inclusion:
- Our program is free to all participants. We do not require proof of income or any other documentation; families simply sign up through the community center. We have intentionally kept the program low-barrier because we know that paperwork and fees can deter families who might benefit the most.
- We currently have several bilingual families (primarily Spanish-English), and we have been slowly working to make our communication more accessible by translating flyers and using simple visuals. The printing support included in this request will help us do that more consistently and professionally.
- We also welcome neurodivergent youth and those with physical disabilities. For instance, one of our 6th-grade participants, Ethan, has sensory sensitivities and initially struggled with the feel of soil on his hands. Over time, with gloves and patience, he found tasks he enjoys, like watering and measuring plant growth, and now proudly tracks the height of "his" sunflowers each week.

Program Leadership and Capacity:
- My name is Melissa J. Kask, and I have lived in Stoughton for 11 years. I am a parent of two children in the Stoughton Area School District and have a background in community education and volunteer coordination. I serve as the primary volunteer coordinator for the garden, scheduling work days, recruiting and supporting volunteers, and handling communication with families.
- While we are largely volunteer-run, we are committed to building systems that make the program sustainable beyond just a few individuals. This is why part of our request includes modest stipends for high school youth mentors, who can gradually take on more responsibility and continuity from year to year.

Use of Funds and Accountability:
- If awarded RoundUP Program funds, we are happy to provide a short written summary at the end of the growing season outlining how the funds were used, how many youth participated, approximate pounds of produce harvested, and a few brief stories or quotes from families.
- We will also acknowledge the RoundUP Program’s support on our garden signage and in any printed or digital communication about the program. Our hope is that community members who see the sign will recognize that their small "round up" contributions are directly supporting something tangible and positive in Stoughton.

Future Vision:
- Looking ahead 2–3 years, we would like to add a small outdoor classroom area (a few benches and a simple shade structure), expand our pollinator garden, and partner with local health professionals to offer basic nutrition and cooking demonstrations using garden produce.
- We see this project as a seed—both literally and figuratively—for a broader culture of community gardening, local food, and youth leadership in Stoughton. Support from the RoundUP Program at this stage would be a meaningful step toward that long-term vision.
